metadata.title: Adaptive Therapy — Managing Cancer Like a Chronic Disease
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one earned accent per beat; mono for code/output only
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Hindi. Wagwan check: charsum('adaptive-therapy-revolution') % 10 == 2, not 0 — and Wagwan is Bear's alone; Liam never takes it.
metadata.note: RETROFIT of the nbb/Onda CLI teardown into the claude-explainer skin, Liam voice. LAWS: B00 = ClaudeComposerAsk cold open, never a brand card (type-on). IN-FOR-BEAR LAW: B00's first breath says 'this is Liam, in for Bear' and the outro signs off the same way. ASK->RESULT LAW: every generated result is preceded by the real Claude prompt that made it (B02 research ask -> B03 evidence; B06 revision ask -> B07 revised table). ILLUSTRATE LAW: the Claude UI appears only where the UI is the subject (asks, code beat, artifact pages, handoff, outro); concept beats are paper screenshots / tables Bear drops into media/ slots. SPARK-LINE LAW: every inner beat pairs the terracotta spark with one short serif line; typing appears in B00 and the handoff only. VERDICT as a one-page ClaudeWindow artifact. HANDOFF second-to-last (greeting 'Your turn.'). OUTRO restates the title. GATE P before any audio spend. SLOTS TO FILL (Bear): media/B01.png (competition schematic or Gatenby figure), media/B03.png (Zhang 2017 Kaplan-Meier PFS curve screenshot), media/B07.png (revised drug-holiday table). RUNNABLE: B02/B06 prompts are real Claude prompts; adaptive_audit.py (B04) runs as-is with python3.
metadata.description_blurb: Standard oncology tries to kill every cancer cell. Adaptive therapy deliberately keeps some alive — and in one prostate-cancer trial it pushed progression-free survival from about 17 months to 27. Liam takes the evidence apart in the Claude cut.

beats.narration_text: Namaste — this is Liam, in for Bear. Standard oncology tries to kill every cancer cell. One trial did the opposite: it left some alive on purpose, and the cancer took longer to come back. Let's find out why.

props.command: why would leaving cancer cells alive beat killing all of them?

beats.narration_text: Maximum-dose therapy kills the drug-sensitive cells first — the easy ones. That clears the field for the resistant cells, which now grow with nothing to compete against. You win the battle and hand the tumor the war.

shot.note: SLOT — Bear fills: schematic of maximum-dose vs adaptive dynamics, or the Gatenby et al. 2009 (Cancer Research) competition figure. Cream stage, terracotta highlight ring on the resistant population. Falls back to a slate if empty.
shot.sparkLine: Kill the sensitive, free the resistant.

beats.narration_text: So we ask for the evidence, with its citations attached — not a summary, the actual trials.

props.command: Research adaptive therapy in cancer: the evolutionary rationale (Gatenby 2009), the Moffitt abiraterone prostate trial (Zhang et al. 2017, Nature Communications), clinical evidence in other cancers, and the practical barriers to adoption. Cite published sources.
props.sparkLine: Ask for the evidence, with citations.

beats.narration_text: And here's the receipt. Moffitt's Phase 2 abiraterone trial: adaptive dosing reached twenty-seven months of progression-free survival against about seventeen for continuous dosing. One prospective, controlled trial — in one cancer.

shot.note: SLOT — Bear fills: screenshot of the Zhang et al. 2017 (Nature Communications 8:1816) Kaplan-Meier PFS curve, adaptive vs continuous. DOCUMENT motion = Remotion highlight/underline keyed to the 27 vs 16.8 figures. Provenance sidecar media/B03.source.txt required.

beats.narration_text: Don't trust the claim — count it yourself. This little script pulls each trial's numbers and its status, then prints the comparison. Runnable as-is: the ones with real trial data print their survival, the rest print no RCT data — which is most of them.

props.title: adaptive_audit.py — the count, not the claim
props.code:
TRIALS = [
    {"setting": "Prostate (abiraterone)", "PFS_mo": 27.0,
     "control_PFS_mo": 16.8, "status": "Phase 2 complete"},
    {"setting": "Breast (endocrine)",     "PFS_mo": None,
     "control_PFS_mo": None, "status": "Preclinical only"},
    {"setting": "GBM (TMZ)",              "PFS_mo": None,
     "control_PFS_mo": None, "status": "Phase 1 recruiting"},
]
for t in TRIALS:
    pfs = (f"{t['PFS_mo']:.1f} vs {t['control_PFS_mo']:.1f} mo"
           if t['PFS_mo'] else "no RCT data")
    print(f"{t['setting']:<26} {pfs:<18} [{t['status']}]")

beats.narration_text: Put the output on one page and the whole field snaps into focus. One column of real numbers, two columns of promise. Adaptive therapy has one strong trial and a lot of preclinical hope.

props.artifactTitle: Adaptive vs continuous — the evidence, one page
artifactLines: Breast (endocrine) — no RCT data · preclinical only
GBM (temozolomide) — no RCT data · Phase 1 recruiting
One controlled trial carries the whole idea. The rest is promise.
props.sparkLine: One trial strong, the rest thin.

beats.narration_text: Now the harder question — the one that decides whether any of this reaches a clinic.

props.command: Add a column for each cancer type: the minimum drug holiday that still preserves competitive suppression. Cite any dose-scheduling trials — and mark the rows where none exist.
props.sparkLine: Now ask the question that decides it.

beats.narration_text: And the new column comes back mostly blank. Outside prostate, nobody knows the safe holiday yet. Because the entire strategy rests on one assumption: that resistant cells pay a fitness cost when the drug is gone. If that cost is real, the sensitive cells win the moment you ease off. If it isn't, you've just given the tumor a rest.

shot.note: SLOT — Bear fills: the revised table image (drug-holiday column, mostly 'unknown' outside prostate) OR the Strobl et al. 2021 (Cancer Research 81:1135) figure on the cost-of-resistance / turnover condition. Terracotta ring on the empty cells. Falls back to a slate if empty.
shot.sparkLine: It all rests on a fitness cost.

beats.narration_text: The verdict, on one page. Adaptive therapy is a shift from cure to control — treat the cancer like a chronic disease you manage, not an enemy you erase. One strong trial says it can work. The fitness-cost assumption is the load-bearing wall. Measure that, and the rest follows.

props.artifactTitle: Adaptive therapy — the teardown, one page
artifactLines: The move: keep sensitive cells alive so they suppress the resistant ones.
The evidence: one controlled trial (prostate, 27 vs 16.8 mo). Everything else is preclinical.
The load-bearing assumption: resistant cells pay a fitness cost off-drug.
The gap: is that cost measured, or inferred from response curves? That's the whole ballgame.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take this prompt and make Claude score the assumption for itself — measured, or inferred?

props.command: Read Zhang et al. 2017 (Nature Communications) and Strobl et al. 2021 (Cancer Research). For each, tell me: is the fitness cost of resistance directly measured, or inferred from response curves? Rate the evidence and show your reasoning.

beats.narration_text: Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. Liam, in for Bear.

props.title: Adaptive therapy.
props.subline: Liam, in for Bear · build it, then take it apart
